benchmark_description: Keep records as appropriate, such as pictorial, written, and/or digital records, of investigations conducted.

lesson.title: How Scientists Keep Records
lesson.intro: Think about the last time you did something really fun — do you remember every detail? Scientists need to remember everything they see during an investigation. That is why they keep records! Records are like a memory bank you can look at forever.
lesson.body_md:
## What Are Science Records?

A **record** is anything you use to save information from your investigation. Records help you:
- Remember what you observed
- Share your findings with others
- Check your work later
- Compare with other investigations

## Types of Records

### 1. Pictorial Records
**Pictorial** means using pictures or drawings. You might draw:
- What a leaf looks like up close
- How tall a plant grew
- What you see through a hand lens

Drawings capture details that are hard to put into words.

### 2. Written Records
**Written** records use words and numbers. Examples:
- Writing '5 cm tall' in your science notebook
- Making a list of materials you used
- Writing what you saw happen step by step

### 3. Digital Records
**Digital** records are saved on a computer, tablet, or camera. Examples:
- Taking a photo of your experiment
- Typing notes on a tablet
- Recording a short video of what happened

## When Do You Record?

You record **during** and **right after** an investigation — not the next day, when you might forget details. Good scientists write things down while they are still watching.

## Organizing Your Records

Your records are most useful when they are organized. A **data table** is a great way to keep numbers organized:

| Day | Plant Height |
|-----|-------------|
| 1   | 2 cm        |
| 3   | 4 cm        |
| 5   | 7 cm        |
| 7   | 10 cm       |

This table makes it easy to see that the plant grew over time.
lesson.worked_example_md:
## Worked Example

**Investigation:** Watch a caterpillar every day and record what you notice.

**Day 1:** Draw a picture of the caterpillar. Write: 'It is green and about 3 cm long. It is eating a leaf.'

**Day 4:** Draw a picture. Write: 'The caterpillar made a cocoon. It is 2 cm long now and attached to a stick.'

**Day 10:** Draw a picture. Write: 'A butterfly came out of the cocoon! It has orange and black wings.'

**Why these records are good:**
- **Pictorial** — drawings show what the caterpillar looked like each day
- **Written** — words give details like color, size, and behavior
- **Organized** — using day numbers makes it easy to see the sequence

Later, these records could be shared with another class who is also watching caterpillars.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Which of these is an example of a **pictorial record** in science?

choices.label: Writing '10 cm' in your notebook
choices.explanation: Writing a number is a written record, not a pictorial one.

choices.label: Drawing a picture of the insect you observed
choices.explanation: A drawing is a pictorial record — it captures what something looks like visually.

choices.label: Telling your friend what you saw
choices.explanation: Telling a friend verbally is communication, but not a record you can look at later.

choices.label: Remembering the color in your head
choices.explanation: A memory in your head is not a record — records are things you write, draw, or save.

practice_questions.explanation_md: A pictorial record is a visual record — a drawing or photo that shows what something looked like. This is different from a written record that uses words and numbers.

practice_questions.prompt_md: When is the BEST time to write down what you observed in an experiment?

choices.label: A week after the experiment
choices.explanation: Waiting a week means you may forget important details.

choices.label: During or right after you observe something
choices.explanation: Recording immediately captures accurate details before memory fades.

choices.label: Before you start the experiment
choices.explanation: You cannot record what you observed before you have done the observing.

choices.label: Only if the teacher reminds you
choices.explanation: Good scientists record automatically — they do not wait to be reminded.

practice_questions.explanation_md: Recording during or immediately after an observation ensures the most accurate details. Memory fades quickly, so writing things down right away is a key scientific habit.

practice_questions.prompt_md: A student takes a photo of her rock collection on a tablet. What kind of record is this?

choices.label: Written record
choices.explanation: A photo does not use words — it is not a written record.

choices.label: Digital record
choices.explanation: A photo taken on a tablet is a digital record — it is saved electronically.

choices.label: Pictorial record drawn by hand
choices.explanation: A photo taken by a camera is digital, not a hand-drawn picture.

choices.label: No record at all
choices.explanation: A photo is absolutely a record — it captures what the rocks looked like.

practice_questions.explanation_md: A photo taken on a tablet is saved digitally — that makes it a digital record. Digital records include photos, videos, and typed notes on electronic devices.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Why is a **data table** useful for recording science results?

choices.label: Because it looks fancy on paper
choices.explanation: Appearance is not the reason for using a data table.

choices.label: Because it organizes information so it is easy to read and compare
choices.explanation: A data table puts numbers in rows and columns so patterns and differences are easy to spot.

choices.label: Because all scientists are required to draw tables by law
choices.explanation: There is no law requiring data tables — scientists use them because they are helpful.

choices.label: Because it takes up more space in your notebook
choices.explanation: Using more space is not the goal — organization is.

practice_questions.explanation_md: A data table organizes information in rows and columns so it is easy to read, compare numbers across days or groups, and spot patterns.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Carlos watched an ice cube melt. He said he would remember everything and did not write anything down. Later he could only remember it took 'a while.' What was his mistake?

choices.label: He watched the ice cube — only adults should do that
choices.explanation: Watching an ice cube is a perfectly fine observation for any scientist.

choices.label: He did not keep a record, so he lost the details he observed
choices.explanation: Without recording, details fade from memory. 'A while' is not useful data — a specific time in minutes would be.

choices.label: He should have used a bigger ice cube
choices.explanation: Ice cube size is not the issue here — recording is the issue.

choices.label: Ice cubes cannot be studied scientifically
choices.explanation: Ice cubes are studied in science all the time — melting is a physical process with measurable properties.

practice_questions.explanation_md: Without a written or drawn record, specific details are lost. 'A while' tells us nothing useful — a note saying '14 minutes' would be real evidence.

practice_questions.prompt_md: A student wrote 'The worm moved fast' in her notebook. Her partner drew a detailed picture of the worm's path. Which record is MORE useful for sharing findings, and why?

choices.label: The written record, because words are always better than pictures
choices.explanation: Words and pictures are both valuable — neither is always better. It depends on what information is needed.

choices.label: The drawing, because it shows the actual path the worm took, which words like 'fast' do not capture
choices.explanation: A drawing of the path gives specific visual information that words like 'fast' cannot provide. Both records together are even better.

choices.label: Neither, because worms are not scientific subjects
choices.explanation: Worms and their behavior are absolutely subjects of scientific study — they are part of life science.

choices.label: The written record, because drawings take too long
choices.explanation: Drawing time is not a reason to prefer written records — the best record captures what actually happened.

practice_questions.explanation_md: A drawing of the worm's path shows exactly where it went, how it curved, and how far it moved — details that 'the worm moved fast' does not capture. Both types of records together are most powerful.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Which of these would be the MOST complete record of a seed growing into a plant over two weeks?

choices.label: One drawing on day 14 only
choices.explanation: A single drawing at the end misses everything that happened along the way.

choices.label: Daily drawings and written notes with measurements every other day
choices.explanation: Regular drawings combined with measurements gives a complete picture of how growth changed over time.

choices.label: A memory of what the plant looked like at the end
choices.explanation: Memory alone is not a reliable record and captures no intermediate steps.

choices.label: One sentence written after the two weeks
choices.explanation: A single sentence at the end misses the details of how the plant changed day by day.

practice_questions.explanation_md: A complete record for a two-week growth study includes regular observations throughout — drawings, measurements, and notes at multiple time points, not just the end.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Maya observed a butterfly and wrote: 'The butterfly had wings.' Her partner wrote: 'The butterfly had two orange wings with black edges, each about 4 cm wide.' Which record is BETTER for science, and why?

choices.label: Maya's, because it is shorter and easier to read
choices.explanation: Shorter is not better in science if it loses important details.

choices.label: Her partner's, because it includes specific details — color, pattern, and size — that are useful as evidence
choices.explanation: Specific observations (color, pattern, size in centimeters) are far more useful than vague ones when comparing or explaining findings.

choices.label: Both are equal because they both mention wings
choices.explanation: They both mention wings, but one captures far more useful scientific detail than the other.

choices.label: Neither, because butterflies should only be recorded with photos
choices.explanation: Written records with detailed observations are perfectly valid and valuable in science.

practice_questions.explanation_md: Scientific records should be specific and detailed. 'Two orange wings with black edges, 4 cm wide' tells us far more than 'had wings' — and those details can be compared with records from other butterflies.

practice_questions.prompt_md: A class recorded weather data for 30 days using written numbers. Later, they wanted to see how temperature changed over the month. Which would BEST show this change?

choices.label: Re-reading all 30 numbers aloud one by one
choices.explanation: Reading 30 numbers out loud makes it very hard to see the pattern over the month.

choices.label: A graph or chart that plots temperature for each day
choices.explanation: A graph turns numbers into a visual shape — making it easy to see whether temperatures went up, down, or stayed the same over 30 days.

choices.label: A single drawing of a sunny day
choices.explanation: One drawing cannot show how temperature changed over 30 days.

choices.label: Asking the teacher to guess the pattern
choices.explanation: The data is already collected — the class should use their own records, not guesses.

practice_questions.explanation_md: Graphs and charts convert tables of numbers into visual displays that make patterns easy to see. A temperature graph over 30 days immediately shows whether it was getting warmer or cooler.

practice_questions.prompt_md: A student recorded her plant heights in a table for 5 days. Another student just memorized theirs. On day 6, they need to write an explanation about how their plants grew. Who can write the BETTER explanation, and why?

choices.label: The one who memorized, because she has it all in her head and does not need paper
choices.explanation: Memory is unreliable for specific numbers over multiple days — the written record is more accurate.

choices.label: The one with the table, because she has specific measurements for each day to use as evidence
choices.explanation: A data table provides exact numbers for each day — those numbers serve as evidence in the explanation and make it far stronger than one based on memory.

choices.label: Both are equal because they both watched their plants
choices.explanation: Watching without recording gives one vague memory; recording gives detailed evidence for all five days.

choices.label: Neither, because plants do not grow fast enough to study in 5 days
choices.explanation: Many plants show measurable growth in just a few days — this is a common science investigation in second grade.

practice_questions.explanation_md: Written records preserve exact data. On day 6, the student with the table can cite specific heights ('on day 1 it was 2 cm; on day 5 it was 10 cm') as evidence, while the one relying on memory can only give vague descriptions.

mastery_check.prompt_md: A student is watching snails move across a wet surface. She wants to record the distance each snail moves in 1 minute. What is the BEST record to keep?

choices.label: Tell her friend the numbers after the experiment is done
choices.explanation: Telling someone later may introduce errors in the numbers — writing them down immediately is more accurate.

choices.label: Write each snail's distance in a table during the investigation
choices.explanation: A written table with numbers for each snail during the investigation is accurate, organized, and easy to compare.

choices.label: Draw only a picture of each snail without recording distances
choices.explanation: A drawing captures appearance but not the distance each snail traveled — measurement data is needed.

choices.label: Guess how far each snail moved after the minute is up
choices.explanation: Guessing instead of measuring is not scientific — you should measure during the investigation.

mastery_check.explanation_md: A written table with measurements taken during the investigation is the best way to record distance data. It is accurate, organized, and ready to share or compare.

mastery_check.prompt_md: A student used three types of records for her investigation: a drawing of her setup, a table of daily temperatures, and a photo at the end. Why is using multiple types of records better than using just one?

choices.label: Because it uses more paper
choices.explanation: Using more paper is not a scientific benefit.

choices.label: Because different types capture different information — visuals, numbers, and sequence — giving a more complete picture
choices.explanation: A drawing shows setup, a table shows number patterns over time, and a photo shows the end result. Together they capture what no single record type could alone.

choices.label: Because scientists are required to use all three types always
choices.explanation: Scientists choose the best record types for their investigation — there is no rule requiring all three every time.

choices.label: Because it is more confusing and scientists like challenges
choices.explanation: More record types should make understanding easier, not harder — organization is key.

mastery_check.explanation_md: Different record types capture different kinds of information. Combined, they give a richer and more complete account of the investigation than any single type could.

mastery_check.prompt_md: After investigating how different soils hold water, a student wants to share her findings with another class. Which record would be MOST useful to share?

choices.label: A memory of what she saw
choices.explanation: Memories cannot be shared as a document — and they may be inaccurate.

choices.label: A data table with the amount of water each soil type held
choices.explanation: A table with specific numbers is organized, easy to read, and can be used by another class for comparison.

choices.label: A drawing of the dirt only
choices.explanation: A drawing of dirt does not show the data about how much water each soil held.

choices.label: Nothing — you cannot share science records
choices.explanation: Sharing science records is exactly what scientists do — communication is a core part of science.

mastery_check.explanation_md: A data table with specific measurements is the most informative record to share — another class can read the numbers, compare them to their own results, and check the findings.

remediation.prompt_md: You are watching seeds sprout. How should you remember what you saw?

choices.label: Draw a picture in your science notebook
choices.explanation: Drawing what you see creates a visual record you can look at later.

choices.label: Just remember it in your head
choices.explanation: Memories fade — a written or drawn record is more reliable.

choices.label: Ask someone else to remember for you
choices.explanation: Other people's memories are also unreliable — you need your own record.

choices.label: Stop watching so you do not forget
choices.explanation: Stopping watching means you miss important observations — keep watching and record at the same time.

remediation.explanation_md: Drawing in your science notebook while you observe creates a permanent pictorial record that you can look at later.

remediation.prompt_md: Which of these is a **written record** of a science observation?

choices.label: A photo taken on a tablet
choices.explanation: A photo is a digital record, not a written record.

choices.label: Writing 'The rock is rough and gray' in your notebook
choices.explanation: Words written in a notebook are a written record.

choices.label: A drawing of the rock
choices.explanation: A drawing is a pictorial record, not a written one.

choices.label: Telling the teacher out loud
choices.explanation: Speaking is communication but not a record — a record is something you can go back and look at.

remediation.explanation_md: A written record uses words and numbers — writing 'The rock is rough and gray' in your notebook is a clear example.

remediation.prompt_md: What should a science notebook have in it?

choices.label: Drawings, measurements, and notes from your investigations
choices.explanation: A science notebook is the place to keep all your observations — drawings, numbers, and written notes.

choices.label: Only your favorite drawings that look nice
choices.explanation: A science notebook is for recording evidence, not just for artwork.

choices.label: Nothing — it is only for writing stories
choices.explanation: A science notebook is for science observations, not for fiction stories.

choices.label: Your lunch order and spelling words
choices.explanation: A science notebook is specifically for recording science observations and data.

remediation.explanation_md: A science notebook holds all of your observations — drawings of what you saw, numbers from measurements, and written notes about what happened.

remediation.prompt_md: Why do scientists draw pictures during an investigation?

choices.label: Because they like art class
choices.explanation: Scientists draw to capture what things look like — it is a tool for recording, not just art.

choices.label: To capture details about what something looks like that are hard to describe with words
choices.explanation: A drawing can show shape, color, and detail that words alone cannot fully capture.

choices.label: To make their notebook look pretty
choices.explanation: Appearance is not the goal — accuracy and detail are.

choices.label: Because writing is too slow
choices.explanation: Scientists use both drawings and writing — each captures different information.

remediation.explanation_md: Drawings capture visual details — shape, texture, color, size relationships — that are difficult to fully describe in words. Both drawings and words together make the best records.

remediation.prompt_md: A student measured a rock every day for 3 days and wrote: Day 1: 5 cm, Day 2: 5 cm, Day 3: 5 cm. What does this record tell her?

choices.label: The rock grew 5 cm each day
choices.explanation: The rock stayed at 5 cm — it did not grow. Rocks do not change size from day to day.

choices.label: The rock stayed the same size all three days
choices.explanation: The record shows the same measurement three days in a row — the rock did not change.

choices.label: She made a mistake because numbers should change
choices.explanation: Numbers staying the same is a perfectly valid result — it shows no change occurred.

choices.label: She only recorded one day
choices.explanation: The record shows three separate days with measurements — that is three days of data.

remediation.explanation_md: The record shows the same measurement (5 cm) on all three days — this is evidence that the rock did not change size. Consistent results are valid and informative.
